does: This paper introduces a method for creating steering vectors that guide large language model behavior toward personalized objectives without full fine-tuning. Rather than directly extracting steering vectors from human preference activations, the authors propose bi-directional preference optimization to train vectors that effectively influence generation probabilities over contrastive preference pairs. The approach operates by adjusting activations in specific transformer layers, enabling lightweight personalization that preserves the original model's general capabilities while steering outputs toward desired behaviors in alignment-sensitive scenarios.
connects: This work builds on recent research in activation-based steering and lightweight LLM adaptation, extending prior methods that extract steering vectors from preference data activations. It responds to limitations in existing steering approaches that directly extract vectors without explicit preference-based optimization, drawing on broader work in reinforcement learning from human feedback and preference learning for language models. The bi-directional optimization framework relates to contrastive learning and alignment techniques that have shown promise in making model behavior more interpretable and controllable while avoiding expensive full-parameter fine-tuning.
enables: This approach enables more robust personalization of LLMs across diverse applications and user preferences, with particular potential in safety-critical and alignment-sensitive domains. The method's improved steering vector quality could facilitate efficient deployment of customized models without retraining, opening possibilities for dynamic adaptation to individual user needs. Future work could extend bi-directional optimization to multi-objective steering, explore interactions between multiple steering vectors, and investigate how steering affects downstream task performance beyond preference alignment, potentially advancing the broader goal of controllable and interpretable large language models.
